tags: 強い vs 弱い pair
lesson:
酒に弱い — weak against alcohol, a lightweight. This pattern — Xに弱い — is one of the most useful things about this word. It means you're susceptible to X, you can't handle it. 暑さに弱い (bad with heat). プレッシャーに弱い (cracks under pressure). The に marks what defeats you.

After a number, 弱 means "slightly less than": 三十分弱 (a little under thirty minutes). Its opposite 強 means "slightly more than": 百人強 (just over 100 people). This is a genuinely useful trick that no dictionary definition of "weak" would prepare you for.

The kanji 弱 shows two bows (弓) with decorative marks — a bow that's ornamental rather than functional. It looks like a weapon but can't shoot. The appearance of strength without the substance.

Dictionaryよわい
Negativeよわくない
Pastよわかった
Past negよわくなかった
Adverbよわく
て-formよわくて

耳が痛い doesn't always mean your ear hurts. It can mean something you heard was painfully true — criticism that hit home. 耳にする (to happen to hear something). 耳が早い (to have fast ears — to hear news quickly, to be well-informed). The ear in Japanese is the organ of being alert, being connected to the world.

The kanji 耳 is one of the most faithful pictographs surviving into modern writing. The oracle bone version was a drawing of an ear, and the modern form still looks like one — the outer rectangle is the ear shape, the inner horizontal lines are the curves of the canal. It's also a radical in other kanji: 聞 (to hear/ask — an ear at a gate), 聴 (to listen carefully).

Sound anchor: mi-mi — the doubled sound is itself worth noting. Japanese has several body-part words with repeated syllables: 耳 (みみ, ear), 目 (め, eye — not doubled, but paired with 耳 constantly). The two senses appear together in phrases like 目と耳を使う (use your eyes and ears — pay close attention).

Before chairs arrived from the West, Japan sat on the floor. 座る originally meant exactly that — settling onto the ground or a cushion, folding your legs underneath you. The kanji 座 shows people (人人) on earth (土) under a roof (广). People sitting on the ground inside a building. That's what sitting looked like for most of Japanese history.

Modern 座る works for chairs too: 椅子に座る (sit in a chair). ここに座ってください (please sit here). But the floor-sitting origin persists in vocabulary: 正座 (せいざ, formal kneeling — "correct sitting"), the legs-folded-underneath position that numbs your feet after ten minutes. 座布団 (ざぶとん, floor cushion) — the cushion that makes floor-sitting bearable.

Particles: 座る takes に — the location where you sit. 椅子に座る. 床に座る (sit on the floor).

Passive に座られる: に marks the sitter. 隣に座られた (someone sat next to me — and I didn't want them to). Adversative passive — their sitting affected you.

Causative を／に座らせる: を leans coercive. 子供を椅子に座らせる (make the kid sit in the chair).

The kanji 右 is a hand (ナ) plus a mouth (口). A hand reaching toward the mouth — and since most people eat with their right hand, that hand became "right." Its twin 左 (ひだり, left) has a hand plus a tool-like shape — the hand that holds instruments. Right eats, left works. The ancient division of labor is frozen in the characters.

右に曲がる (turn right). 右手 (みぎて, right hand). 右側 (みぎがわ, right side). In Japan, traffic drives on the left, pedestrians keep left on escalators in Osaka (but right in Tokyo — a regional quirk that confuses even Japanese people). Direction words get daily use.

右 in compounds read as う carries political or metaphorical weight: 右翼 (うよく, right wing — political). 左右 (さゆう, left and right — and also "influence," as in 結果を左右する, to influence an outcome, to sway it left and right).

浴びる doesn't mean "to take a bath" — that's おふろに入る. 浴びる means to have something pour over you, to be drenched in it. The most common use: シャワーを浴びる (take a shower). But it extends beyond water: 日光を浴びる (bathe in sunlight). 注目を浴びる (bathe in attention — be in the spotlight). 批判を浴びる (be showered with criticism).

The pattern is always the same: something pours down on you and you receive it with your whole body. Water, light, praise, blame — the image is of standing under a cascade. The kanji 浴 has 氵 (water) and 谷 (valley) — water flowing down from a valley onto you.

Particles: 浴びる takes を — the thing pouring over you is the direct object. シャワーを浴びる. 批判を浴びる.

Potential が浴びられる: が. ここで朝日が浴びられる (you can catch the morning sun here).

Passive に浴びせられる: more commonly, the transitive 浴びせる (to pour onto someone) is used in passive. 水を浴びせられた (water was poured on me).

Causative に…を浴びさせる: に marks who you drench, を marks what. 子供にシャワーを浴びさせる (have the kid take a shower).

肩がこる — stiff shoulders. This might be the most culturally loaded body complaint in Japan. Where Americans get back pain and Germans get circulation problems, Japanese people get 肩こり (shoulder stiffness). It's discussed so universally that some linguists have argued the Japanese experience shoulder tension more because they have a word for it — the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis in action, or just a nation that spends a lot of time hunched over desks.

肩の荷が下りる (the load on my shoulders came down) — a burden was lifted. 肩を持つ (hold someone's shoulder) — to take someone's side. 肩身が狭い (narrow-shouldered feeling) — to feel ashamed or out of place. Shoulders in Japanese carry responsibility, allegiance, and social comfort.

The kanji 肩 has 戸 (door) on top and the flesh radical 月 below. The shoulder as the body's door-frame — the flat platform where arms hinge. Architecturally apt.

もう寝る — I'm going to bed. Three syllables, conversation over, goodnight. 寝る covers both going to sleep and lying down. 昨日は早く寝た (went to bed early). ソファで寝ていた (was lying on the sofa — maybe asleep, maybe just horizontal).

寝る vs 眠る: 寝る is the physical act — getting into bed, lying down. 眠る (ねむる) is the state of being asleep, the unconsciousness itself. You can 寝る without 眠る (lie in bed unable to sleep). You can 眠る while sitting up on a train. 寝ても眠れない (I'm in bed but I can't sleep) uses both to make the distinction explicit.

One more: 寝坊する (ねぼうする, to oversleep) — a compound you'll need. 寝 (sleep) + 坊 (a suffix that implies someone behaving like a child). Oversleeping as childish indulgence.

Particles: 寝る is intransitive. Use で for where: ベッドで寝る. No direct object.

Passive に寝られる: adversative — someone's sleeping affects you. 赤ちゃんに横で寝られて動けない (the baby fell asleep next to me and I can't move).

Causative を／に寝させる: 子供を早く寝させる (put the kid to bed early).

消す makes things disappear. 電気を消す (turn off the light). テレビを消す (turn off the TV). 火を消す (put out a fire). 文字を消す (erase text). データを消す (delete data). The common thread: something that was present — light, sound, flame, information — stops existing. You remove it from the world.

Its intransitive partner 消える (きえる) is when things vanish on their own: 電気が消えた (the light went out). 雪が消えた (the snow melted away). You 消す — you cause the disappearance. Something 消えるs — it fades by itself.

Particles: 消す takes を — the thing you extinguish is the direct object. 電気を消す.

Potential が消せる: が. このデータが消せない (I can't delete this data).

Passive に消される: に. 大事なファイルを消された (my important file was deleted).

Causative に…を消させる: に marks who you make erase, を marks what. 生徒に黒板を消させる (have the student erase the blackboard).

元気でね — take care. Said when someone is leaving for a long time, carrying a warmth that "goodbye" can't match. It literally means "be well" — stay in your original energy, keep being alive in that buzzing way. This single phrase captures what 元気 really means: not just "fine" but vitally, actively well.

元 (origin) + 気 (energy/spirit) = original energy. The baseline life force. 元気な子供 is a kid who won't sit still. 元気がない is the opposite — something's wrong, the spark is out. 元気を出す (muster energy — pull yourself together, cheer up).

The 気 half of this word is one of the most connected kanji in Japanese. 天気 (weather — the sky's energy). 気持ち (feelings — where energy is held). 人気 (popularity — people's energy gathered around something). 気分 (mood — the portion of energy you're feeling right now). These aren't random compounds. They're all variations on a single idea: qi, the invisible force that moves through everything. 元気 is that force at its source.

Dictionaryげんき
Negativeげんきじゃない
Pastげんきだった
Past negげんきじゃなかった
Adverbげんきに
て-formげんきで

全部食べた (ate everything). 全部でいくら？ (how much for everything?). 全部 is the blunt, no-remainder "all" — every single part, nothing left out. It works both as a noun and as an adverb, and it's the word you reach for when you want to emphasize totality.

The kanji: 全 (all, complete) + 部 (part, section). All parts. The word is reassuringly literal. 全 shows up in other totality words: 全然 (ぜんぜん, not at all / completely), 全員 (ぜんいん, everyone — all members), 全国 (ぜんこく, nationwide).

全部 vs 全て vs みんな: All mean "everything/all," different flavors. 全部 is the most concrete — every piece, every item. 全て (すべて) is more literary and abstract — "all things." みんな usually means "everyone" (people) but can mean "everything" casually. For physical objects and countable things, 全部 is the default.

去 means "to leave, to depart." 年 means "year." The year that left — last year. Clean, visual, immediate. 去年の夏 (last summer). 去年はどうでしたか (how was last year?).

Note the prefix pattern breaks here. For weeks and months, you use 先 for "last" (先週, 先月). For years, it's 去 instead. There's no 先年 in common use. This is one of those places where the system isn't perfectly regular — you just have to know that years are different.

The 去 kanji appears in 過去 (かこ, the past) — literally "passed and departed." Once something is 去, it's gone. Not coming back.

The kanji 引 is a bow (弓) with a vertical line — the string pulled taut. Drawing a bowstring is the original act of pulling, and the kanji preserves it perfectly.

引く does more work than English "pull." ドアを引く (pull a door). 辞書を引く (look up in a dictionary — "pull out" information). 風邪を引く (catch a cold — you "draw" the illness to yourself). 線を引く (draw a line). 引く手あまた (more offers than you can handle — everyone pulling at you). The thread across all uses: bringing something toward you, or drawing something out.

The opposite is 押す (おす, to push). You'll see 引 and 押 on doors everywhere — 引く to pull open, 押す to push open. And 引っ越す (ひっこす, to move house) literally means to pull up and cross over — uprooting yourself.

Particles: 引く takes を — the thing you pull is the direct object. ドアを引く. 辞書を引く.

Potential が引ける: が. この戸が引けない (I can't pull this door open).

Passive に引かれる: に marks who pulls. 彼の才能に引かれた (I was drawn to his talent).

Causative に…を引かせる: に marks who pulls, を marks what. 馬に荷車を引かせる (have the horse pull the cart).

The 館 suffix is your "building" marker: 映画館 (えいがかん, movie theater), 美術館 (びじゅつかん, art museum), 旅館 (りょかん, Japanese inn). Once you spot 館, you know you're talking about a building with a purpose. 図書館 is 図書 (books as a collection) + 館 (hall). A hall of books.

図書 isn't the word for a single book (that's 本). It implies a collection, an archive — institutional books. 図書カード (library card). 図書室 (としょしつ) is a smaller version — a library room in a school, using 室 (room) instead of 館 (hall).

図書館で勉強する (study at the library) is a staple of student life. 図書館の本を借りる (borrow a library book) — note 借りる (to borrow), not 貸す (to lend). You borrow; the library lends.

上げる has two major lives. First: to physically raise something. 手を上げる (raise your hand). Its intransitive partner 上がる (あがる) is when something rises on its own: 温度が上がる (the temperature rises). You 上げる things. Things 上がる by themselves.

Second life: to give (to someone of equal or lower status). プレゼントをあげる (give a present). And in the 〜てあげる pattern, it means doing something as a favor: 教えてあげる (I'll teach you — as a kindness). This giving sense is so common many learners meet it before the "raise" meaning.

The two aren't disconnected. Giving is an upward motion — you lift something from your side and hand it over. In classical Japanese, offering to a superior meant literally raising it up to them. The physical gesture and the social act share a direction.

Particles: For raising: を. 手を上げる. For giving: に marks the recipient, を marks the gift. 友達にお菓子をあげる.

Potential が上げられる: が. 給料が上げられない (can't raise the salary).

Passive に上げられる: に. 税金を上げられた (taxes were raised on us).

Causative に…を上げさせる: に marks who raises, を marks what. 生徒に手を上げさせる (have students raise their hands).

Traffic lights in Japan are called 青信号 — "blue" signal. They're green. 青野菜 — "blue" vegetables. They're green. 青々とした草 — lush "blue" grass. Definitely green. What's happening: historically, Japanese 青 covered the entire blue-to-green spectrum. 緑 existed as a separate word, but green was considered a shade of blue, not its own category.

The boundary has been shifting toward the English distinction over the past century, but the old system lingers everywhere. And 緑 is a noun, not an adjective — you can't say 緑い. To modify a noun, use 緑の: 緑の葉 (green leaves). This puts it in a different grammatical class from 赤い, 青い, 白い, 黒い — all い-adjectives. Green didn't earn its own adjective form because it wasn't considered a basic color.

The kanji has the silk radical 糸 — green was a key dye color for silk and fabric. 緑茶 (りょくちゃ, green tea) uses the on'yomi りょく. 新緑 (しんりょく, the fresh green of new spring leaves) is a seasonal word that evokes a specific time of year.

腕がいい doesn't mean "nice arms." It means "skilled." 腕を磨く (polish your arms) means to hone your skills. 腕の見せどころ (a place to show your arms) means a chance to show what you can do. 腕前 (うでまえ) means ability level. Your arms are your tools, and what you do with them defines your capability. The figurative meaning is at least as common as the physical one.

For the literal body part: うで means the arm from shoulder to wrist. 腕時計 (うでどけい, wristwatch — arm-clock). 腕組み (うでぐみ, folding your arms) — a gesture of thinking, skepticism, or defiance, much as in Western body language.

Three words for "door," each for a different kind. ドア: Western-style, hinged, swings open — houses, cars, building entrances. 戸 (と): traditional Japanese, slides — the default in older-style rooms. 扉 (とびら): large, formal — double doors, grand entrances, or metaphorical doors ("the door to a new era"). Your bedroom probably has a ドア. The tatami room has a 戸 or ふすま.

ドアを開ける (open the door). ドアを閉める (close the door). ドアをノックする (knock on the door) — ノック is another English loan. Western doors came with Western verbs for using them.

女 (woman) + の + 子 (child) = a female child. Transparent construction. But the age range stretches: a group of women in their twenties might casually call themselves 女の子たち. It carries warmth and youthfulness that 女性 (じょせい, woman — formal) and 女 (おんな, woman — blunt) don't.

Choosing wrong has consequences: 女の子 (warm, youthful) → 女の人 (おんなのひと, neutral polite) → 女性 (じょせい, formal, respectful) → 女 (おんな, direct, can sound rude). When in doubt, 女性 is safe. Calling a grown woman 女の子 can be affectionate or patronizing depending on context. Calling her 女 flatly is almost always too blunt.

The kanji 男 is 田 (rice field) over 力 (power/strength). Power in the rice field. For thousands of years, this was the definition of maleness across East Asia — not warrior strength but agricultural labor, the muscle that feeds the family. The character hasn't changed, even as the job description has.

男の子 works the same way as 女の子: 男 + の + 子 = a male child, a boy. Like its counterpart, the age range is flexible. かわいい男の子 could describe a toddler or a twenty-year-old, depending on who's talking. The word is inherently warm and light — never formal, never harsh.

Register matters: 男の子 (casual, affectionate) → 男の人 (おとこのひと, neutral polite) → 男性 (だんせい, formal) → 男 (おとこ, blunt, rough). The formal 男性 uses the Chinese-derived reading だん; the casual words use native おとこ. In Japanese, Chinese readings almost always signal higher formality. Keep that principle and you can predict register across hundreds of words.

Japanese makes plurals by adding suffixes. 私 (I) + たち = we. Same たち works on anything: 子供たち (children), 彼たち (they). The word 友達 (friend) already has 達 — the kanji form of たち — baked in.

たち isn't the only option. ら is rougher: 僕ら (ぼくら, we — masculine casual), 俺ら (おれら, we — masculine rough). たち is softer, polite, works everywhere.

And just like 私 alone, 私たち gets dropped when the group reference is obvious. Japanese prefers implicit subjects. 明日行く over 私たちは明日行く when it's clear you mean the group. Using 私たち explicitly adds emphasis — "we are the ones going" — or claims collective identity: 私たちの学校 (our school).
